title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a campsite manager called Ben talking about work placements at an eco-campsite. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a campsite manager called Ben talking about work placements at an eco-campsite. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A campsite manager called Ben talks about work placements at an eco-campsite.
extracts.audio_script: Hi. I’m Ben, manager of Pine Lake Eco-Campsite, and I’m here to tell you about our summer work placements. The campsite is next to woodland and a small lake, but it was not originally created as a tourist business. The main reception building started as a [9]forestry office[/9], used by workers looking after the surrounding trees. When the woodland became a public recreation area, part of the land was redesigned for campers who wanted simple accommodation in a natural setting.||Students on placement spend one week with us and work alongside our permanent staff. Visitors can bring ordinary tents or stay in wooden huts. The huts attract a lot of bookings, but our most frequently requested type of accommodation this year is [10]bell tents[/10], which are already set up before guests arrive and are popular with families. Before any duties begin, students attend a short meeting. The most important part is training in [11]fire safety[/11], as cooking areas and campfires must be managed carefully, especially during dry weather.||Placement students help with several daily activities. They may check campsite maps, prepare visitor information or work outdoors. On the first morning, they usually assist with [12]recycling collection[/12], emptying clearly labelled containers and learning how we reduce waste across the site. We provide work gloves and a uniform sweatshirt. Students need to bring a [13]reusable bottle[/13], since we try to avoid selling single-use plastic drinks. It can be refilled at safe water points around the campsite throughout the day. Comfortable outdoor shoes are essential as paths are natural and sometimes muddy.||Pine Lake aims to show visitors that holidays can have a low environmental impact. Solar lights are used on paths, and guests borrow boxes for sorting waste. Our newest improvement is a set of [14]compost toilets[/14] near the woodland camping field, which use far less water than ordinary facilities. Students may explain these to visitors, although maintenance is completed by trained staff. We also offer activities. Last summer, canoe sessions filled quickly, but this year families have shown most interest in [15]wildlife walks[/15], guided by a local expert who helps visitors identify birds, insects and woodland signs.||At the end of their week, each placement student meets the supervisor and receives a written [16]skills summary[/16], outlining punctuality, teamwork and tasks completed. Former students often return for our annual [17]lantern evening[/17], when safe lights mark paths around the lake and musicians perform near reception. This event also raises funds. Our next plan is to improve access for visitors who cannot walk comfortably over rough ground. Money collected this year will contribute to an [18]electric buggy[/18], allowing staff to transport guests and luggage from reception to the camping areas. Applications should be completed by the end of April.

dialogue.speaker: ben
dialogue.text: Hi. I’m Ben, manager of Pine Lake Eco-Campsite, and I’m here to tell you about our summer work placements. The campsite is next to woodland and a small lake, but it was not originally created as a tourist business. The main reception building started as a forestry office, used by workers looking after the surrounding trees. When the woodland became a public recreation area, part of the land was redesigned for campers who wanted simple accommodation in a natural setting.||Students on placement spend one week with us and work alongside our permanent staff. Visitors can bring ordinary tents or stay in wooden huts. The huts attract a lot of bookings, but our most frequently requested type of accommodation this year is bell tents, which are already set up before guests arrive and are popular with families. Before any duties begin, students attend a short meeting. The most important part is training in fire safety, as cooking areas and campfires must be managed carefully, especially during dry weather.||Placement students help with several daily activities. They may check campsite maps, prepare visitor information or work outdoors. On the first morning, they usually assist with recycling collection, emptying clearly labelled containers and learning how we reduce waste across the site. We provide work gloves and a uniform sweatshirt. Students need to bring a reusable bottle, since we try to avoid selling single-use plastic drinks. It can be refilled at safe water points around the campsite throughout the day. Comfortable outdoor shoes are essential as paths are natural and sometimes muddy.||Pine Lake aims to show visitors that holidays can have a low environmental impact. Solar lights are used on paths, and guests borrow boxes for sorting waste. Our newest improvement is a set of compost toilets near the woodland camping field, which use far less water than ordinary facilities. Students may explain these to visitors, although maintenance is completed by trained staff. We also offer activities. Last summer, canoe sessions filled quickly, but this year families have shown most interest in wildlife walks, guided by a local expert who helps visitors identify birds, insects and woodland signs.||At the end of their week, each placement student meets the supervisor and receives a written skills summary, outlining punctuality, teamwork and tasks completed. Former students often return for our annual lantern evening, when safe lights mark paths around the lake and musicians perform near reception. This event also raises funds. Our next plan is to improve access for visitors who cannot walk comfortably over rough ground. Money collected this year will contribute to an electric buggy, allowing staff to transport guests and luggage from reception to the camping areas. Applications should be completed by the end of April.

questions.explanation: Ben explains that reception was originally a 'forestry office'.

questions.explanation: Ben says 'bell tents' are currently most requested.

questions.explanation: Ben says students first help with 'recycling collection'.

questions.explanation: Ben asks students to bring a 'reusable bottle'.

questions.explanation: Ben states that the new facilities are 'compost toilets'.

questions.question: The activity attracting most family interest is (15) .
questions.explanation: Ben says families are most interested in 'wildlife walks'.

Fundraising will help pay for an (18) for guests and luggage.
